questions.question: The adrenal glands are located in which retroperitoneal space?
questions.detail: The perirenal space contains the kidneys, proximal ureters, and adrenals.

questions.question: Which artery is NOT one of the three primary sources of arterial blood to the adrenal gland?
questions.detail: Each gland gets blood from three arteries: superior (from inferior phrenic), middle (from aorta), and inferior (from renal artery).

questions.question: The right adrenal vein drains directly into the IVC, while the left adrenal vein typically drains into the:
questions.detail: Venous drainage is via one main vein: the right into the IVC and the left into the left renal vein.

questions.question: Which zone of the adrenal cortex is responsible for producing Aldosterone?
questions.detail: The Zona Glomerulosa makes Aldosterone; prolonged stimulation here leads to hypertrophy.

questions.question: The Zona Fasciculata is the layer of the adrenal cortex that produces:
questions.detail: The Zona Fasciculata makes Cortisol.

questions.question: Which part of the adrenal gland is responsible for making catecholamines (e.g., Epinephrine)?
questions.detail: The medulla makes catecholamines.

questions.question: In the third trimester of pregnancy, the relative size of the adrenal gland compared to the kidney is:
questions.detail: In the 3rd Trimester the adrenal is 1/3 the size of the kidney; by adulthood, it is 1/13 the size.

questions.question: On neonatal ultrasound, the adrenal gland has a 'triple stripe' or 'Oreo' appearance due to:
questions.detail: The triple stripe appearance consists of a hypoechoic cortex and a hyperechoic medulla.

questions.question: An elongated flat 'Pancake' or 'Discoid' adrenal gland is a sign of:
questions.detail: An elongated flat (pancake) adrenal is seen with congenital absence of the kidney.

questions.question: A 'Horseshoe Adrenal' occurs when the limbs of the glands fuse in the midline and is associated with:
questions.detail: Horseshoe adrenal glands are associated with asplenia (right isomerism).

questions.question: What is the most common cause of an adrenal 'mass' in a neonate?
questions.detail: Adrenal hemorrhage is the most common cause of an adrenal mass in a neonate.

questions.question: In the setting of neonatal adrenal hemorrhage, 70% of cases occur on which side?
questions.detail: 70% of cases occur on the right due to compression between the liver and kidney.

questions.question: On ultrasound, a neonatal adrenal hemorrhage characteristically appears:
questions.detail: Hemorrhage should be anechoic and avascular.

questions.question: What is the 'Classic Next Step' to differentiate neonatal adrenal hemorrhage from a cystic neuroblastoma?
questions.detail: Serial ultrasounds can differentiate them: hemorrhage will get smaller over time; cancer will not.

questions.question: 'Waterhouse-Friderichsen Syndrome' refers to adrenal hemorrhage in the setting of:
questions.detail: This syndrome involves adrenal hemorrhage during fulminant meningitis.

questions.question: Adrenal Hyperplasia on ultrasound is described as a 'big adrenal' that looks like:
questions.detail: Hyperplasia classically shows a wrinkled surface ('looks like a brain').

questions.question: Congenital adrenal hypertrophy is caused by 21-hydroxylase deficiency in > 90% of cases and manifests as:
choices: Genital ambiguity in girls or salt-losing pathology in boys
questions.detail: 21-Hydroxylase deficiency results in genital ambiguity or salt-losing.

questions.question: The association of 'Bilateral Solid Testicular Masses' and 'Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia' points to:
questions.detail: Bilateral testicular masses + CAH = Adrenal Rests.

questions.question: 'Cushing Disease' is specifically defined as an overproduction of ACTH caused by:
questions.detail: Cushing Disease is overproduction of ACTH by a pituitary adenoma.

questions.question: Which of the following is a classic 'Adrenal Mass Mimic' in a neonate?
questions.detail: Infradiaphragmatic extralobar sequestration is a classic mimic of an adrenal mass.

questions.question: Adrenal Adenomas are the most common adrenal tumor; what is the Hounsfield Unit (HU) threshold on non-contrast CT to 'call it an adenoma'?
choices: Adenomas are always fatty on CT
questions.detail: On non-contrast CT, an adrenal mass measuring less than 10 HU is diagnostic for adenoma.

questions.question: To calculate Absolute Washout for an adrenal mass, the formula is:
choices: It is based on MRI signal only
questions.detail: Absolute Washout uses the formula: (Enhanced - Delayed) / (Enhanced - Unenhanced) x 100.

questions.question: An Absolute Washout value of greater than what percentage is diagnostic for an adrenal adenoma?
questions.detail: Absolute Washout > 60% = Adenoma.

questions.question: A Relative Washout value of greater than what percentage is diagnostic for an adrenal adenoma?
questions.detail: Relative Washout > 40% = Adenoma.

questions.question: Which hypervascular metastasis can mimic adenoma washout and should be suspected if portal venous phase HU > 120?
questions.detail: Hypervascular mets (RCC/HCC) can mimic washout; look for HU > 120 on portal venous phase.

questions.question: On MRI, the hallmark of an adrenal adenoma is:
choices: Signal drop-out on Out-of-Phase T1 imaging
questions.detail: Adenomas show signal drop-out on In and Out-of-Phase T1.

questions.question: 'Conn's Syndrome' is characterized by excessive aldosterone production and is most commonly caused by:
questions.detail: Conn's Syndrome is most commonly caused by a benign adenoma (70%).

questions.question: 'Pheochromocytomas' are characteristically associated with which MRI signal finding?
questions.detail: Pheos are characteristically T2 bright.

questions.question: The extra-adrenal location for a pheochromocytoma, typically found at the level of the IMA, is the:
questions.detail: Extra-adrenal pheos are often at the Organ of Zuckerkandl (near the IMA).

questions.question: According to the 'Rule of 10s' for pheochromocytomas, what percentage are bilateral?
questions.detail: The Rule of 10s states that 10% are extra-adrenal, 10% bilateral, 10% in children, and 10% hereditary.

questions.question: Which genetic syndrome is MOST associated with multiple and bilateral pheochromocytomas?
questions.detail: MEN 2 is associated with multiple and bilateral pheochromocytomas in 50% of patients.

questions.question: The 'Carney Triad' includes extra-adrenal pheochromocytoma, GIST, and:
questions.detail: The Carney Triad consists of Extra-Adrenal Pheo, GIST, and Pulmonary Chondroma.

questions.question: An adrenal mass that contains macroscopic 'bulk fat' on CT is a:
questions.detail: A Myelolipoma is a benign tumor that contains bulk fat.

questions.question: Adrenal Myelolipomas can produce a 'speed displacement artifact' on ultrasound, making the diaphragm appear:
questions.detail: Fat causes sound to travel slower, creating a discontinuous diaphragm artifact.

questions.question: Adrenal 'Cortical Carcinoma' is typically suspected when a mass is:
choices: LARGE (4 cm - 10 cm) with central necrosis
questions.detail: Cortical Carcinoma is typically LARGE (4-10 cm) and often has central necrosis.

questions.question: 'Wolman Disease' is a total Aunt Minnie described as:
questions.detail: Wolman Disease is a fat metabolism error with bilateral enlarged calcified adrenals.

questions.question: Adrenal metastases are common; which imaging finding helps distinguish them from lipid-poor adenomas?
questions.detail: Portal venous phase HU > 120 suggests a met or pheo rather than an adenoma.

questions.question: Which nuclear medicine agent is first-line for evaluating neuroblastoma and pheochromocytoma?
questions.detail: MIBG is first-line for pheo, paraganglioma, and neuroblastoma.

questions.question: Before performing an MIBG scan, which organ must be blocked to prevent unintended radiation?
questions.detail: You must block the thyroid prior to MIBG.

questions.question: If you see a 'skeleton' (bone uptake) on an MIBG scan, it indicates:
questions.detail: Skeleton on MIBG = Diffuse bone mets.

questions.question: Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN) 1 is associated with the '3 Ps': Pituitary, Pancreas, and:
questions.detail: MEN 1 = 3 Ps (Pituitary, Parathyroid, and Pancreas).

questions.question: Which pancreatic tumor is most commonly seen in MEN 1?
questions.detail: Gastrinoma is the most common pancreatic/duodenal tumor in MEN 1.

questions.question: MEN 2a is associated with Medullary Thyroid Cancer, Pheochromocytoma, and:
questions.detail: MEN 2a = 1M, 2Ps (Medullary Thyroid Ca, Pheochromocytoma, Parathyroid).

questions.question: MEN 2b is distinguished from 2a by the LACK of parathyroid involvement and the presence of:
questions.detail: MEN 2b features Medullary Thyroid Ca, Pheo, Mucosal Neuroma, and Marfanoid habitus.

questions.question: 'Carcinoid Syndrome' (flushing, diarrhea, right heart failure) typically does NOT occur until:
choices: The lesion metastasizes to the liver
questions.detail: The syndrome does not occur until liver mets are present (the liver normally metabolizes serotonin).

questions.question: What is the 'most common' primary location for a GI carcinoid tumor?
questions.detail: The distal ileum is the most common primary site.

questions.question: The lab test used to screen for Carcinoid Syndrome is:
questions.detail: Urine 5-HIAA is the test for Carcinoid.

questions.question: Systemic serotonin from Carcinoid Syndrome characteristically degrades which heart valve?
questions.detail: Carcinoid classically causes right-sided valve disease, specifically tricuspid regurgitation.

questions.question: Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) syndrome is associated with hemangioblastomas (retina/cord), RCC, and:
questions.detail: VHL patients have a 10% risk for Pheochromocytoma.

questions.question: 'Tuberous Sclerosis' features a clinical triad of Facial Angiofibroma, Seizures, and:
questions.detail: The TS triad is facial angiofibromas, seizures, and mental retardation.

questions.question: Which endocrine tumor is a hallmark of MEN 2 (both a and b) and occurs in 100% of MEN 2a cases?
questions.detail: Medullary Thyroid Cancer is a constant in MEN 2.

questions.question: 'Intestinal Ganglioneuromas' are associated with which syndrome?
questions.detail: MEN 2b is associated with intestinal ganglioneuromas.

questions.question: Elevated levels of Calcitonin in MEN 2 can cause symptoms similar to Carcinoid syndrome, such as:
questions.detail: Elevated calcitonin in Medullary CA causes flushing and diarrhea.

questions.question: In MEN 1, what is the most common PITUITARY tumor?
questions.detail: Prolactinoma is the most common pituitary tumor in MEN 1.

questions.question: Which syndrome features multiple head and neck paragangliomas and a risk for pheochromocytoma?
questions.detail: PGL Syndrome involves multiple head and neck paragangliomas.

questions.question: 'Zinner Syndrome' links male infertility with renal agenesis and:
questions.detail: Zinner Syndrome = renal agenesis + seminal vesicle cyst.

questions.question: 'Kallman Syndrome' is a rare cause of male infertility associated with:
questions.detail: Kallman Syndrome = can't smell + infertile.

questions.question: 'Klinefelter Syndrome' (47XXY) features tall stature, infertility, and a high risk for:
questions.detail: Klinefelter patients have a 300x risk for GCTs in their chest.

questions.question: In the 'Adrenal Ultrasound Cases' strategy, if a mass is big and does NOT get smaller over time, the diagnosis is:
questions.detail: Neuroblastoma is big and does NOT get smaller over time (unlike hemorrhage).

questions.question: The 'Oreo' sign of the normal neonatal adrenal gland is lost in which condition?
questions.detail: In Hyperplasia, you may lose the central hyperechoic stripe (Oreo filling).

questions.question: The thyroid gland descends from which anatomical landmark during development?
questions.detail: The thyroid descends from the foramen cecum at the base of the tongue.

questions.question: The 'Zuckerkandl Tubercle' is a posterior nodular extension of the thyroid that helps locate the:
questions.detail: It helps with locating the recurrent laryngeal nerve (which is medial to it).

questions.question: Which imaging finding is considered the 'hallmark' or buzzword for Papillary Thyroid Cancer?
questions.detail: Microcalcifications are the hallmark of papillary CA.

questions.question: 'Comet Tail' artifact seen within a thyroid nodule on ultrasound is characteristic of:
questions.detail: 'Comet Tail' artifact is seen in colloid nodules.

questions.question: A thyroid nodule that is 'Cold' on an I-123 scan has what percentage chance of being cancer?
questions.detail: Cold nodules have cancer about 15% of the time.

questions.question: 'Subacute (De Quervain's) Thyroiditis' characteristically presents in a female with:
choices: A painful gland after an upper respiratory infection
questions.detail: Subacute thyroiditis features a painful gland after a cold.

questions.question: During the acute phase of De Quervain's Thyroiditis, radiotracer uptake (RAIU) will be:
choices: Only seen in the pyramidal lobe
questions.detail: RAIU will be decreased during the acute phase of subacute thyroiditis.

questions.question: 'Riedel’s Thyroiditis' is an IgG4-associated disease characterized by:
choices: Thyroid replaced by fibrous tissue (causing compression/dysphagia)
questions.detail: Riedel's involves fibrous replacement and compression of adjacent structures.

questions.question: On MRI, Riedel’s Thyroiditis will appear:
choices: Dark on all sequences (like a fibroma)
questions.detail: It will be dark on all MRI sequences.

questions.question: What is the most common location for ectopic thyroid tissue (90% of cases)?
questions.detail: Lingual Thyroid (back of the tongue) is the most common.

questions.question: 'Graves Disease' is an autoimmune condition where antibodies are directed at the:
questions.detail: Graves involves an antibody directed at the TSH receptor.

questions.question: The classic ultrasound appearance of Graves disease is an enlarged gland that is:
questions.detail: The gland is 'inferno hot' on Doppler.

questions.question: In Graves disease, nuclear medicine I-123 uptake values are typically:
questions.detail: Uptake is typically increased (50-80%).

questions.question: 'Hashimoto's Thyroiditis' is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in the US and is associated with:
choices: Increased risk of primary thyroid lymphoma
questions.detail: Hashimotos has an increased risk of primary thyroid lymphoma.

questions.question: The 'White Knight' sign on thyroid ultrasound refers to:
choices: Uniform hyperechoic regenerative nodules in Hashimoto's
questions.detail: 'White Knights' are uniform hyperechoic nodules seen in Hashimotos.

questions.question: 'Delphian Nodes' are level 6 lymph nodes that, if 'sick-looking,' suggest:
questions.detail: For multiple choice purposes, a sick level 6 node is a laryngeal cancer met.

questions.question: Thyroid cancer metastases to the brain are unique because they tend to be:
questions.detail: Thyroid cancer is hypervascular and can bleed like stink in the brain.

questions.question: When thyroid cancer spreads to the lungs, the classic pattern on imaging is:
questions.detail: The classic pattern for lung mets is 'miliary'.

questions.question: A risk of treating diffuse lung thyroid metastases with high-dose I-131 is:
questions.detail: Pulmonary fibrosis is a risk when treating diffuse lung mets with I-131.

questions.question: Which thyroid cancer subtype does NOT respond to I-131 therapy because it does not concentrate iodine?
questions.detail: Medullary thyroid CA does NOT drink I-131.

questions.question: For a 'First-trimester' patient with hyperthyroidism, the recommended drug is:
questions.detail: PTU is recommended during pregnancy.

questions.question: The 'Wolff-Chaikoff Effect' refers to:
choices: Reduction in thyroid levels caused by ingestion of a large amount of iodine
Excessive growth of the thyroid during pregnancy
questions.detail: The W.C. effect is a suppression of the gland by large amounts of iodine.

questions.question: What is the NRC limit for residual activity before a thyroid patient can be released from the hospital?
questions.detail: The number to remember is 33 mCi of residual activity.

questions.question: Following high-dose I-131 therapy, how long should a patient wait before getting pregnant?
questions.detail: No getting pregnant for 6-12 months after therapy.

questions.question: If you see an Iodine scan with uptake in the LIVER, it is always:
choices: A post-treatment scan (representing metabolized thyroid hormone)
questions.detail: Uptake in the liver on an Iodine scan is ALWAYS a post-treatment scan.

questions.question: How many parathyroid glands are normally present in an adult?
questions.detail: There are normally 4 parathyroid glands.

questions.question: The superior two parathyroid glands originate from which embryological structure?
questions.detail: Superior 2 are from the 4th pouch; inferior 2 are from the 3rd pouch.

questions.question: Which parathyroid glands are MORE likely to be in an ectopic location (e.g., in the chest)?
questions.detail: The inferior two are more likely to be ectopic.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of hyperparathyroidism (90%)?
questions.detail: Hyperfunctioning Adenoma (85-90%) is the leading cause.

questions.question: On ultrasound, a parathyroid adenoma typically appears as:
choices: Hypoechoic beans posterior to the thyroid
questions.detail: Adenomas look like hypoechoic beans.

questions.question: On a 4D-CT scan, a parathyroid adenoma characteristically demonstrates:
questions.detail: 4D-CT shows early enhancement and delayed washout.

questions.question: Parathyroid Carcinoma is rare (1%) and is distinguished from an adenoma on imaging by:
choices: Cervical adenopathy or invasion of adjacent structures
questions.detail: The only way to tell carcinoma on imaging is invasion or adenopathy.

questions.question: Localization of parathyroid adenomas using Tc-99m Sestamibi imaging depends on:
questions.detail: Sestamibi imaging depends on mitochondrial density and blood flow.

questions.question: In the 'Dual Phase' Sestamibi technique, parathyroid pathology is identified because:
choices: The tracer sticks around longer (delayed uptake) while thyroid tissue washes out
It is only seen on the 24-hour scan
questions.detail: Uptake remains in abnormal parathyroid on the delayed phase.

questions.question: Which of the following can cause a 'False Positive' on a Sestamibi parathyroid scan?
choices: Thyroid nodules, Head and Neck cancers, or Lymphadenopathy
questions.detail: These three things like to 'drink' Sestamibi and mimic adenomas.

questions.question: In the 'Dual Tracer' technique, the second agent (e.g., I-123) is used because it:
choices: Goes to both thyroid and parathyroid
Only goes to the thyroid (allowing for subtraction)
questions.detail: The second agent only goes to the thyroid.

questions.question: Successful surgical treatment of hyperparathyroidism is defined as an intra-operative reduction of PTH by:
questions.detail: Success is defined as a 50% reduction in PTH.

questions.question: If you see 'Lymph Nodes' on a Sestamibi (MIBI) scan, the strategy says you should:
choices: Consider them suspicious (maybe cancer) and eval with ultrasound
Ignore them if they are in the neck
questions.detail: On a MIBI study, you should NOT see lymph nodes; if you do, they are suspicious.

questions.question: Which parathyroid condition is associated with 8-10% of cases and often involves all four glands?
questions.detail: Multi-Gland Hyperplasia accounts for about 10% of cases.

questions.question: Parathyroid tissue can be surgically auto-transplanted into which location to maintain function after total removal?
questions.detail: Glands can be implanted into the forearm.
